title: Al Forthan Scholarship — Full Essay Set
html:
The 40KB master document for the Al Forthan Scholarship — Volunteers of America Oregon's scholarship for children of parents struggling with addiction. Six long-form essays plus extensive research notes pulled from FHS 215: (1) teen drug-abuse solutions, (2) race/class and incarceration, (3) the personal alcoholism narrative, (4) lessons learned, (5) why college matters, (6) miscellaneous reflections. The most policy-research-heavy doc in the entire 2021 scholarship essay cycle — Yahir cites NSDUH data, names Harmony Academy as the model intervention, and analyzes the reagan era war on drugs prison-population explosion (329K → 627K).

TL;DR

  • Application for the Al Forthan Scholarship — VOA Oregon's award for kids of addicted parents — and the most ambitious essay set in Yahir's 2021 cycle, blending personal narrative with policy research.
  • Cites NSDUH stat "1 in 8 children in homes with substance-abuse history will bear the same fate" and breaks out gender risk multipliers (4-9x males, 2-3x females).
  • Argues for treating addiction as a health problem rather than a crime: replace probation/parole with counseling, fund recovery high schools, make intervention accessible to teens.
  • Names the reagan era war on drugs as the policy origin of mass-incarceration disparity — "rich college white boy with cocaine gets a slap on the wrist, the black teen father gets 25 years for an ounce of marijuana."
  • The personal-narrative section (essay 3) deepens the alcoholism arc beyond what appears in OSU/Ford/JCEF — explicitly names "becoming the protector of my household," the eldest-son burden, and the gradual "slurring monster" transformation he watched.
  • Yahir later wins this scholarship — see 2021 05 06 5PM APRIL 21 Speech Al Forthan for the acceptance speech.

Key quote

"It hurts to watch my industrious and loving father slowly blend into the villain of my story."

Why it matters in the graph

This is the deepest personal-narrative doc in the 2021 cycle and the single best source for Yahir's policy fluency on addiction and incarceration — every other essay in this cycle either summarizes from this one or borrows its phrasing. Connecting node between alcoholism in the family, criminal justice policy, and al forthan. Pair with 2021 01 17 FHS 215 CJ JJ F2020 (the academic source) and 2021 05 06 5PM APRIL 21 Speech Al Forthan (the won-the-scholarship payoff).

Open questions

  • Did Yahir keep any contact with Al Forthan / VOA Oregon after the scholarship — did the "community that believes in me" framing actualize?
  • The forgiveness-arc closure ("my dad has been tackling sobriety") — what's the 2024-26 update?

Connections

  • 2021 05 06 5PM APRIL 21 Speech Al Forthan — the won-it speech 3 months later.
  • 2021 01 17 FHS 215 CJ JJ F2020 — academic source for policy claims.
  • al forthan · harmony academy · reagan era · war on drugs · mass incarceration · addiction as health problem · criminal justice policy · alcoholism in the family · scholarship essay cycle
